questions.question: Can an abstract class have a constructor?
options: No — abstract classes cannot be instantiated so constructors are useless
Yes — subclasses call it via super() during their construction
Yes — but only a no-arg constructor
No — constructors are only for concrete classes
questions.explanation: Abstract classes can and often do have constructors. Subclasses call them via super() to initialize inherited state. The abstract class itself cannot be instantiated directly.

questions.question: What happens if a concrete subclass does NOT implement all abstract methods from its abstract parent?
options: The unimplemented methods return null by default
Runtime error when the methods are called
Compile error — the subclass must also be declared abstract
The parent's implementation is used
questions.explanation: If a subclass doesn't implement all abstract methods, it must itself be declared abstract. Otherwise the compiler reports an error.

questions.question: In the Template Method pattern, why is the template method typically declared `final`?
options: To improve performance
To prevent subclasses from changing the algorithm's structure
Because abstract classes require final methods
To allow multiple inheritance
questions.explanation: Making the template method final ensures subclasses can customize individual steps (abstract methods) but cannot change the overall algorithm flow.

questions.question: Which statement about abstract methods is correct?
options: Abstract methods can have a method body
Abstract methods can be private
Abstract methods have no body and end with a semicolon
Abstract methods are optional in abstract classes
questions.explanation: Abstract methods declare only the signature — no body, terminated with a semicolon. They cannot be private because subclasses need to see and override them.

questions.question: When should you prefer an abstract class over an interface?
options: When you need multiple inheritance
When subclasses need to share state (fields) and partial implementation
When you want to define a functional interface
When you need static utility methods
questions.explanation: Abstract classes are preferred when subclasses share instance fields and concrete methods. Interfaces cannot hold instance state — only constants.
